Just now

--

Write the document for the reader that actually shows up. All images generated by author unless statedWe spent two years learning to write prompts for the AI and documentation for the humans. Turns out we had the reader backwards.
A last year I wrote a book about prompting. It did what books do: a person reads it, absorbs the ideas and applies them to the AI sitting in the next tab.
Then a small, annoying thought arrived and wouldn’t leave. I’d written the whole thing for a human to read and then relay to the machine. But the machine is the thing doing the work. Every idea in that book was going to reach the AI eventually, just slowly, second-hand and filtered through whatever the reader happened to remember, misread or quietly skip.
So why route it through a person at all? Why not write it for the reader that actually shows up?
The reader we keep ignoring
Look at how most of us hand knowledge to AI today. We write onboarding docs, playbooks, brand guidelines and process notes, all for people. Then, when the AI needs to do the work, we paste a hurried paragraph into a chat box and hope it infers the other ninety percent.
We’ve split our knowledge into two piles: careful documentation the AI never reads and throwaway prompts that carry all the load. The most important reader in the building is working…
